summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that software business owners should always refund small amounts to unhappy customers without argument, using a real example from Dropsend where the owner escalated a $5 refund request into a hostile exchange. He contends that suppressing your ego and immediately resolving the complaint costs almost nothing compared to the revenue lost from an angry customer who cancels or files a chargeback. McKenzie advocates adopting a pre-committed strategy of obsequiousness in customer service, drawing on the Japanese concept of tatemae. He dismisses common objections like company refund policies and slippery-slope fears, noting that as a business owner you can override any policy at will. The core message is that you can never win an argument with a customer, so don't start one.
key_points: A $5 refund costs far less than losing a $50/year revenue stream plus potential chargeback fees and stress
Pre-commit to a strategy of immediate resolution to prevent escalation — decide beforehand to be obsequious
You are the boss of your own refund policy; override it whenever the accommodation advances business goals
The slippery slope argument ('if I do it for one I'll have to do it for everyone') is a fiction — customers mostly talk about great or terrible service, not how to exploit you
Suppressing your ego feels hard for about two weeks, then becomes habit and protects your mental health long-term
money_quotes: The only thing that matters is you keeping your cool, and this can frequently require having the patience of Job and less ego than a blade of grass.
You can win an argument with your boss. You can win an argument with your wife. You can even win an argument with God. But you will never win an argument with your customer.

In the pursuit of a lousy five stinking bucks you've just lost a $50 revenue stream over the next year, which is almost pure profit because you are selling a software service which requires no marginal work.
I often wish English had a word for when you need to be a spineless craven lickspittle in a good way.
Here is the great thing about running your own business: you are the boss. If your company has a policy against refunds, you can break it at will.
Your customers are an incestuous bunch but they mostly don't talk about how easy it is to screw you over.
If you learn how to do this, you can work in a call center for your entire life and not get tired. If you don't, you will burn out in a matter of months.
A nice prominent guarantee like 'We'll refund your last payment anytime, for any reason.' gets seen by every prospect who is worried 'Hmm, what if I'm not happy?'
key_insight: Winning an argument with a customer is impossible — the only winning move is to immediately resolve their complaint, because the cost of ego is always higher than the cost of a refund.
